title: A note on superconvergence in projection-based numerical approximations of eigenvalue problems for Fredholm integral operators
summary: This paper studies the eigenvalue problem $K ψ= λψ$ associated with a Fredholm integral operator $K$ defined by a smooth kernel. The focus is on analyzing the convergence behaviour of numerical approximations to eigenvalues and their corresponding spectral subspaces. The interpolatory projection methods are employed on spaces of piecewise polynomials of even degree, using $2r+1$ collocation points that are not restricted to Gauss nodes. Explicit convergence rates are established, and the modified collocation method attains faster convergence of approximation of eigenvalues and associated eigenfunctions than the classical collocation scheme. Moreover, it is shown that the iteration yields superconvergent approximations of eigenfunctions. Numerical experiments are presented to validate the theoretical findings.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes convergence rates for eigenvalue approximations of Fredholm integral operators using interpolatory projection methods with piecewise polynomials of even degree, showing modified collocation achieves faster convergence than classical schemes.
AI.motivation: To analyze and improve convergence behavior of numerical approximations for eigenvalues and spectral subspaces of Fredholm integral operators with smooth kernels, addressing limitations of classical collocation methods.
AI.method: Uses interpolatory projection methods on spaces of piecewise polynomials of even degree with 2r+1 collocation points (not restricted to Gauss nodes). Implements modified collocation method and analyzes convergence rates theoretically.
AI.result: Establishes explicit convergence rates, shows modified collocation method achieves faster convergence for eigenvalues and eigenfunctions than classical scheme, and demonstrates iteration yields superconvergent eigenfunction approximations. Numerical experiments validate theoretical findings.
AI.conclusion: The modified collocation method with interpolatory projections on piecewise polynomial spaces provides superior convergence properties for eigenvalue problems of Fredholm integral operators, offering practical improvements over traditional approaches.

title: Highly Efficient Rank-Adaptive Sweep-based SI-DSA for the Radiative Transfer Equation via Mild Space Augmentation
summary: Low-rank methods have emerged as a promising strategy for reducing the memory footprint and computational cost of discrete-ordinates discretizations of the radiative transfer equation (RTE). However, most existing rank-adaptive approaches rely on rank-proportional space augmentation, which can negate efficiency gains when the effective solution rank becomes moderately large. To overcome this limitation, we develop a rank-adaptive sweep-based source iteration with diffusion synthetic acceleration (SI-DSA) for the first-order steady-state RTE. The core of our method is a sweep-based inner-loop iterative low-rank solver that performs efficient rank adaptation via mild space augmentation. In each inner iteration, the spatial basis is augmented with a small, rank-independent number of basis vectors without truncation, while a single truncation is performed only after the inner loop converges. Efficient rank adaptation is achieved through residual-based greedy angular subsampling strategy together with incremental updates of projection operators, enabling non-intrusive reuse of existing transport-sweep implementations. In the outer iteration, a DSA preconditioner is applied to accelerate convergence. Numerical experiments show that the proposed solver achieves accuracy and iteration counts comparable to those of full-rank SI-DSA while substantially reducing memory usage and runtime, even for challenging multiscale problems in which the effective rank reaches 30-45% of the full rank.

AI.tldr: A rank-adaptive sweep-based source iteration with diffusion synthetic acceleration (SI-DSA) for radiative transfer equation that uses efficient rank adaptation via mild space augmentation and greedy angular subsampling, achieving significant memory and runtime savings while maintaining accuracy.
AI.motivation: Existing low-rank methods for radiative transfer equation suffer from efficiency loss when solution rank becomes moderately large due to rank-proportional space augmentation. Need more efficient rank adaptation that maintains computational benefits even for higher effective ranks.
AI.method: Develops rank-adaptive sweep-based source iteration with DSA. Uses inner-loop low-rank solver with mild space augmentation (small, rank-independent basis vector additions without truncation during inner iterations). Employs residual-based greedy angular subsampling and incremental projection operator updates. Single truncation only after inner loop convergence. Outer iteration uses DSA preconditioner for acceleration.
AI.result: Achieves accuracy and iteration counts comparable to full-rank SI-DSA while substantially reducing memory usage and runtime. Effective for challenging multiscale problems where effective rank reaches 30-45% of full rank.
AI.conclusion: Proposed method overcomes limitations of existing rank-adaptive approaches by enabling efficient rank adaptation through mild space augmentation and greedy subsampling, making low-rank methods practical even for problems with moderately large effective ranks.

title: How unconstrained machine-learning models learn physical symmetries
summary: The requirement of generating predictions that exactly fulfill the fundamental symmetry of the corresponding physical quantities has profoundly shaped the development of machine-learning models for physical simulations. In many cases, models are built using constrained mathematical forms that ensure that symmetries are enforced exactly. However, unconstrained models that do not obey rotational symmetries are often found to have competitive performance, and to be able to \emph{learn} to a high level of accuracy an approximate equivariant behavior with a simple data augmentation strategy. In this paper, we introduce rigorous metrics to measure the symmetry content of the learned representations in such models, and assess the accuracy by which the outputs fulfill the equivariant condition. We apply these metrics to two unconstrained, transformer-based models operating on decorated point clouds (a graph neural network for atomistic simulations and a PointNet-style architecture for particle physics) to investigate how symmetry information is processed across architectural layers and is learned during training. Based on these insights, we establish a rigorous framework for diagnosing spectral failure modes in ML models. Enabled by this analysis, we demonstrate that one can achieve superior stability and accuracy by strategically injecting the minimum required inductive biases, preserving the high expressivity and scalability of unconstrained architectures while guaranteeing physical fidelity.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces metrics to measure symmetry content in unconstrained ML models for physics, analyzes how symmetry information is learned, and shows that minimal inductive biases can improve stability and accuracy while preserving expressivity.
AI.motivation: While constrained models enforce physical symmetries exactly, unconstrained models often achieve competitive performance and can learn approximate equivariant behavior through data augmentation. The paper aims to understand how symmetry information is processed in unconstrained models and establish a framework to diagnose symmetry-related failures.
AI.method: The authors introduce rigorous metrics to measure symmetry content in learned representations and assess equivariant condition accuracy. They apply these metrics to two unconstrained transformer-based models: a graph neural network for atomistic simulations and a PointNet-style architecture for particle physics. They analyze how symmetry information is processed across architectural layers and learned during training.
AI.result: The analysis reveals how symmetry information is processed in unconstrained models and enables diagnosis of spectral failure modes. The authors demonstrate that strategically injecting minimal required inductive biases can achieve superior stability and accuracy while preserving the high expressivity and scalability of unconstrained architectures.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes a rigorous framework for analyzing symmetry content in ML models for physics, showing that minimal inductive biases can balance physical fidelity with model expressivity, enabling both accuracy and scalability in unconstrained architectures.

title: Permeation of hydrogen across graphdiyne: molecular dynamics vs. quantum simulations and role of membrane motion
summary: Previous research based on electronic structure calculations and molecular dynamics (MD) simulations have demonstrated that graphdiyne (GDY) is a very suitable two-dimensional membrane for the separation of small molecules in a gas mixture of different species. However, quantum effects may play a role in the dynamics of these permeation processes when light molecules are the ones involved in the crossing of the GDY subnanometric pores. In this work we report rigorous quantum-mechanical calculations together with equivalent MD simulations of the transport of H2 molecules through a static GDY membrane, as a case study for the validity of the application to these problems of classical dynamics. The force fields employed are based on an improved Lennard-Jones formulation, with parameters optimized by means of accurate ab initio calculations. It is found that, although quantum effects are still significant at the temperatures of interest (between 250 and 350 K), MD simulations are able to reasonably reproduce the dependence of the quantum permeances with the temperature. Moreover, MD permeances computed with quantum corrections through Feynman-Hibbs effective potentials provide a lower bound to quantum permeances, while the pure classical counterpart gives an upper bound, thus leading to a well delimited range of confidence of the permeation results. Furthermore, within MD simulations it is possible to incorporate the thermal motion of the GDY layer and in this situation it is observed an enhancement of the permeances with respect to the fixed membrane case, due to a significant reduction of the permeation barriers when the GDY atoms are allowed to vibrate. It seems apparent therefore, that modeling the membrane motion is crucial to provide reliable simulations of the gas transport features.

AI.tldr: Quantum effects in H2 permeation through graphdiyne membranes are significant but MD simulations with quantum corrections provide reasonable bounds, while membrane thermal motion enhances permeation.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether classical molecular dynamics (MD) simulations are valid for studying light molecule permeation through graphdiyne membranes, given that quantum effects may play a significant role in these processes.
AI.method: Combined rigorous quantum-mechanical calculations with equivalent MD simulations of H2 transport through static graphdiyne membranes, using improved Lennard-Jones force fields with parameters optimized via accurate ab initio calculations, and incorporating quantum corrections through Feynman-Hibbs effective potentials.
AI.result: Quantum effects remain significant at relevant temperatures (250-350K), but MD simulations reasonably reproduce temperature dependence of quantum permeances. MD with quantum corrections provides lower bounds while pure classical MD gives upper bounds, creating a confidence range. Thermal motion of the membrane enhances permeation by reducing barriers.
AI.conclusion: Classical MD simulations with appropriate quantum corrections can provide reliable bounds for permeation studies, but modeling membrane thermal motion is crucial for accurate gas transport simulations as it significantly affects permeation barriers.

title: An efficient compact splitting Fourier spectral methods for computing the dynamics of rotating spin-orbit coupled spin-2 Bose-Einstein condenstates
summary: This paper investigates the dynamics of spin-2 Bose-Einstein condensates (BECs) with rotation and spin-orbit coupling (SOC). In order to better simulate the dynamics, we present an efficient high-order compact splitting Fourier spectral method. This method splits the Hamiltonian into a linear part, which consists of the Laplace, rotation and SOC terms, and a nonlinear part that includes all the remaining terms. The wave function is well approximated by the Fourier spectral method and is numerically accessed with discrete Fast Fourier transform (FFT). For linear subproblem, the handling of rotation term and SOC term poses a major challenge. Using a function mapping based on rotation, we can integrate the linear subproblem exactly and explicitly. This mapping we propose not only helps eliminate the rotation term, but also prevents the SOC term from evolving into a time-dependent form. The nonlinear subproblem is integrated analytically in physical space. Such "compact" splitting involves only two operators and facilitates the design of high-order splitting schemes. Our method is spectrally accurate in space and high order in time. It is efficient, explicit, unconditionally stable and simple to implement. In addition, we derive some dynamical properties and carry out a systematic study, including accuracy and efficiency tests, dynamical property verification, the SOC effects and dynamics of vortex lattice.

AI.tldr: Efficient high-order compact splitting Fourier spectral method for simulating spin-2 BEC dynamics with rotation and SOC, featuring spectral spatial accuracy and high-order temporal accuracy.
AI.motivation: Need to better simulate dynamics of spin-2 Bose-Einstein condensates with rotation and spin-orbit coupling, requiring efficient numerical methods to handle complex Hamiltonian structure.
AI.method: Compact splitting method that separates Hamiltonian into linear (Laplace, rotation, SOC) and nonlinear parts. Uses Fourier spectral method with FFT, function mapping to handle rotation and SOC terms exactly, and analytical integration of nonlinear subproblem in physical space.
AI.result: Method achieves spectral accuracy in space and high-order accuracy in time. It is efficient, explicit, unconditionally stable, and simple to implement. Successfully applied to study SOC effects and vortex lattice dynamics.
AI.conclusion: The proposed compact splitting Fourier spectral method provides an effective numerical framework for studying spin-2 BEC dynamics with rotation and SOC, enabling systematic investigation of complex physical phenomena.

title: A Reaction-Advection-Diffusion Model to describe Non-Uniformities in Colorimetric Sensing using Thin Porous Substrates
comment: The manuscript has 35 pages, and 11 figures. The supplementary information has 8 pages, and 3 figures. Currently submitted to "Langmuir"
summary: Non-uniform product (color) distribution in colorimetric paper-based sensors affects the accuracy and reliability of measurements. The underlying mechanisms responsible for this are still unclear. The coffee ring effect explains the ring-formation at the periphery. However, ring-like patterns can also be found at intermediate radial positions in these sensors. In this work, we study the influence of mass transport and reaction dynamics within porous/paper substrates on the spatial product distribution. We consider one reactant embedded in a porous substrate, which reacts with another delivered through a sessile droplet. The process is modeled in two stages. In Stage 1, droplet imbibition creates two distinct flow domains in the substrate with moving boundaries. Stage 2 commences after complete penetration. Species-substrate interactions are addressed by including a mobility factor. The developed model is used to analyze the effects of different parameters on the product distribution for two configurations, Reagent-Embedded (RE) and Analyte-Embedded (AE). Our work demonstrates ring-like patterns can form even without evaporation effects. With decreasing analyte-reagent concentration ratio, the profile shifts inward. Thicker, more porous substrates yield greater uniformity but reduce color intensity. Immobilization of embedded species enhances uniformity in RE configuration with mobile product, and in AE configuration with immobile product. The model is validated with lead and nitrite detection experiments for RE and AE configurations respectively. It successfully captures three spatial color variations observed experimentally. This study also explains the emergence of multiple rings in these systems. The insights gained are useful for optimizing sensor design and protocols for colorimetry

AI.tldr: Paper studies non-uniform color distribution in paper-based sensors, showing ring-like patterns can form without evaporation due to mass transport and reaction dynamics in porous substrates.
AI.motivation: Non-uniform product distribution affects accuracy of colorimetric paper-based sensors. While coffee ring effect explains peripheral rings, intermediate ring-like patterns remain unexplained, requiring understanding of mass transport and reaction dynamics in porous substrates.
AI.method: Developed two-stage model: Stage 1 - droplet imbibition creates two flow domains with moving boundaries; Stage 2 - after complete penetration. Includes species-substrate interactions via mobility factor. Analyzed Reagent-Embedded (RE) and Analyte-Embedded (AE) configurations. Validated with lead and nitrite detection experiments.
AI.result: Ring-like patterns form without evaporation. Profile shifts inward with decreasing analyte-reagent concentration ratio. Thicker, more porous substrates increase uniformity but reduce color intensity. Immobilization enhances uniformity in specific configurations. Model captures three spatial color variations observed experimentally and explains multiple rings.
AI.conclusion: Mass transport and reaction dynamics within porous substrates significantly influence spatial product distribution in paper-based sensors. The model provides insights for optimizing sensor design and protocols, explaining formation of ring-like patterns and multiple rings without evaporation effects.

title: Homogenization and operator estimates for Steklov problems in perforated domains
summary: Let the set $Ω_\varepsilon$ be obtained from the bounded domain $Ω$ by removing a family of $\varepsilon$-periodically distributed identical balls. In $Ω_\varepsilon$ one considers the standard Steklov spectral problem. It is known from [Girouard-Henrot-Lagacé, ARMA (2021)] that, if the radii of the holes shrink at a critical rate such that the surface area of a single hole is comparable to the volume of a periodicity cell, then, in the limit $\varepsilon \to 0$, the Steklov spectrum converges to the spectrum of the problem $-Δu=λQ u$ on $Ω$ with some weight $Q>0$. In the present work, we extend this result by proving, under fairly general assumptions on the locations and shapes of the holes, convergence of the associated resolvent operators in the operator norm topology, together with quantitative estimates for the Hausdorff distance between the spectra. The underlying domain $Ω$ is not assumed to be bounded.

AI.tldr: The paper studies convergence of Steklov spectral problems on perforated domains with periodically distributed holes shrinking at a critical rate, extending previous results to more general hole configurations and proving operator norm convergence with quantitative spectral estimates.
AI.motivation: To extend previous results on Steklov spectrum convergence from periodic hole distributions to more general configurations, and to prove stronger convergence results (operator norm convergence) with quantitative estimates for spectral approximation.
AI.method: Analyzes Steklov spectral problems on domains Ω_ε obtained by removing ε-periodically distributed holes from Ω. Considers critical scaling where hole surface area is comparable to periodicity cell volume. Uses spectral analysis techniques to study convergence as ε→0.
AI.result: Proves convergence of resolvent operators in operator norm topology for general hole locations and shapes. Provides quantitative estimates for Hausdorff distance between spectra. Extends results to unbounded domains Ω.
AI.conclusion: The Steklov spectral problem on perforated domains with critically shrinking holes converges to a weighted Laplacian problem, with strong operator convergence and quantitative spectral estimates under general assumptions on hole configurations.

title: Enabling ab initio geometry optimization of strongly correlated systems with transferable deep quantum Monte Carlo
summary: A faithful description of chemical processes requires exploring extended regions of the molecular potential energy surface (PES), which remains challenging for strongly correlated systems. Transferable deep-learning variational Monte Carlo (VMC) offers a promising route by efficiently solving the electronic Schrödinger equation jointly across molecular geometries at consistently high accuracy, yet its stochastic nature renders direct exploration of molecular configuration space nontrivial. Here, we present a framework for highly accurate ab initio exploration of PESs that combines transferable deep-learning VMC with a cost-effective estimation of energies, forces, and Hessians. By continuously sampling nuclear configurations during VMC optimization of electronic wave functions, we obtain transferable descriptions that achieve zero-shot chemical accuracy within chemically relevant distributions of molecular geometries. Throughout the subsequent characterization of molecular configuration space, the PES is evaluated only sparsely, with local approximations constructed by estimating VMC energies and forces at sampled geometries and aggregating the resulting noisy data using Gaussian process regression. Our method enables accurate and efficient exploration of complex PES landscapes, including structure relaxation, transition-state searches, and minimum-energy pathways, for both ground and excited states. This opens the door to studying bond breaking, formation, and large structural rearrangements in systems with pronounced multi-reference character.

AI.tldr: A framework combining transferable deep-learning variational Monte Carlo with Gaussian process regression for accurate ab initio exploration of potential energy surfaces, enabling study of bond breaking and structural rearrangements in strongly correlated systems.
AI.motivation: Faithful description of chemical processes requires exploring extended regions of molecular potential energy surfaces, which is challenging for strongly correlated systems with pronounced multi-reference character. Current methods struggle with direct exploration of molecular configuration space due to the stochastic nature of deep-learning VMC.
AI.method: Combines transferable deep-learning variational Monte Carlo with cost-effective estimation of energies, forces, and Hessians. Continuously samples nuclear configurations during VMC optimization to obtain transferable descriptions. Uses Gaussian process regression to aggregate noisy VMC data from sparse sampling of the PES, constructing local approximations for efficient exploration.
AI.result: Achieves zero-shot chemical accuracy within chemically relevant distributions of molecular geometries. Enables accurate and efficient exploration of complex PES landscapes including structure relaxation, transition-state searches, and minimum-energy pathways for both ground and excited states.
AI.conclusion: The framework opens the door to studying bond breaking, formation, and large structural rearrangements in systems with pronounced multi-reference character, providing a powerful tool for ab initio exploration of potential energy surfaces in strongly correlated chemical systems.

title: Lattice and PT symmetries in tensor-network renormalization group: a case study of a hard-square lattice gas model
comment: 21 pages, 9 figures, and 3 tables; open source code published on GitHub
summary: The tensor-network renormalization group (TNRG) is an accurate numerical real-space renormalization group method for studying phase transitions in both quantum and classical systems. Continuous phase transitions, as an important class of phase transitions, are usually accompanied by spontaneous breaking of various symmetries. However, the understanding of symmetries in the TNRG is well-established mainly for global on-site symmetries like U(1) and SU(2). In this paper, we demonstrate how to incorporate lattice symmetries (including reflection and rotation) and the PT symmetry in the TNRG in two dimensions (2D) through a case study of the hard-square lattice gas with nearest-neighbor exclusion. This model is chosen because it is well-understood and has two continuous phase transitions whose spontaneously-broken symmetries are lattice and PT symmetries. Specifically, we write down proper definitions of these symmetries in a coarse-grained tensor network and propose a TNRG scheme that incorporates these symmetries. We demonstrate the validity of the proposed method by estimating the critical parameters and the scaling dimensions of the two phase transitions of the model. The technical development in this paper has made the 2D TNRG a more well-rounded numerical method.

AI.tldr: The paper extends tensor-network renormalization group (TNRG) to incorporate lattice symmetries (reflection/rotation) and PT symmetry in 2D, demonstrated through the hard-square lattice gas model with two continuous phase transitions.
AI.motivation: While TNRG is established for global on-site symmetries like U(1) and SU(2), there's limited understanding of how to incorporate lattice symmetries and PT symmetry in 2D TNRG. This gap prevents TNRG from being a comprehensive method for studying phase transitions involving these symmetry breakings.
AI.method: The authors develop a TNRG scheme that incorporates lattice symmetries (reflection and rotation) and PT symmetry in 2D. They define proper symmetry representations in coarse-grained tensor networks and apply this to the hard-square lattice gas model with nearest-neighbor exclusion, which has two continuous phase transitions involving these symmetry breakings.
AI.result: The proposed method successfully estimates critical parameters and scaling dimensions for the two phase transitions in the hard-square lattice gas model, demonstrating the validity of incorporating lattice and PT symmetries in 2D TNRG.
AI.conclusion: The technical development makes 2D TNRG a more comprehensive numerical method by enabling the study of phase transitions involving spontaneous breaking of lattice and PT symmetries, complementing existing capabilities for global on-site symmetries.

title: General-Purpose Machine-Learned Potential for CrCoNi Alloys Enabling Large-Scale Atomistic Simulations with First-Principles Accuracy
summary: CrCoNi medium-entropy alloys exhibit exceptional mechanical properties arising from pronounced chemical complexity, including short-range order (SRO), and low stacking fault energy, posing challenges for large-scale atomistic simulations. While most models focus on equimolar compositions, deviations from equimolarity provide an effective route to tuning properties, requiring transferable interatomic potentials that capture composition-dependent behavior. Here we develop a general-purpose machine-learned interatomic potential for the CrCoNi system within the neuroevolution potential (NEP) framework, achieving near first-principles accuracy with high computational efficiency. Trained on a comprehensive dataset spanning pure elements, binary and ternary alloys across a wide compositional range, diverse crystal structures and thermodynamic conditions, and based on spin-polarized \textit{ab initio} data, the model accurately reproduces equations of state, phonons, elastic constants, dislocation dissociation, surface and defect energies, melting temperatures and strain-induced phase transformations. It further captures SRO and its effect on stacking fault energies across both equimolar and non-equimolar compositions, in agreement with first-principles and experiments. In contrast to existing potentials, typically limited to equimolar alloys and less accurate for pure elements, the present model delivers consistent accuracy across the full compositional space while retaining superior efficiency. These results enable reliable atomistic simulations of composition-dependent behaviour and provide a framework for the design of non-equimolar CrCoNi alloys.

AI.tldr: A machine-learned neuroevolution potential (NEP) for CrCoNi alloys achieves near-first-principles accuracy across full compositional range, enabling reliable atomistic simulations of composition-dependent behavior including short-range order and stacking fault energies.
AI.motivation: CrCoNi medium-entropy alloys have exceptional mechanical properties influenced by chemical complexity and composition variations, but existing atomistic models are limited to equimolar compositions and lack transferability across the full compositional space needed for property tuning.
AI.method: Developed a general-purpose machine-learned interatomic potential within the neuroevolution potential (NEP) framework, trained on comprehensive spin-polarized ab initio data spanning pure elements, binary and ternary alloys across wide compositional ranges, diverse crystal structures, and thermodynamic conditions.
AI.result: The model accurately reproduces equations of state, phonons, elastic constants, dislocation dissociation, surface/defect energies, melting temperatures, strain-induced phase transformations, and captures short-range order effects on stacking fault energies across both equimolar and non-equimolar compositions.
AI.conclusion: This transferable NEP potential enables reliable atomistic simulations of composition-dependent behavior in CrCoNi alloys and provides a framework for designing non-equimolar alloys with tuned properties, overcoming limitations of existing equimolar-only potentials.

title: Adaptive Subspace Modeling With Functional Tucker Decomposition
summary: Tensors provide a structured representation for multidimensional data, yet discretization can obscure important information when such data originates from continuous processes. We address this limitation by introducing a functional Tucker decomposition (FTD) that embeds mode-wise continuity constraints directly into the decomposition. The FTD employs reproducing kernel Hilbert spaces (RKHS) to model continuous modes without requiring an a-priori basis, while preserving the multi-linear subspace structure of the Tucker model. Through RKHS-driven representation, the model yields adaptive and expressive factor descriptions that enable targeted modeling of subspaces. The value of this approach is demonstrated in domain-variant tensor classification. In particular, we illustrate its effectiveness with classification tasks in hyperspectral imaging and multivariate time series analysis, highlighting the benefits of combining structural decomposition with functional adaptability.

AI.tldr: Functional Tucker decomposition (FTD) embeds continuity constraints into tensor decomposition using RKHS, enabling adaptive modeling of continuous data without predefined basis functions.
AI.motivation: Traditional tensor discretization obscures important information when data originates from continuous processes, creating a need for decomposition methods that preserve mode-wise continuity.
AI.method: Introduces functional Tucker decomposition (FTD) using reproducing kernel Hilbert spaces (RKHS) to model continuous modes without requiring a-priori basis, while preserving Tucker model's multi-linear subspace structure.
AI.result: FTD yields adaptive and expressive factor descriptions enabling targeted subspace modeling, demonstrated effective in domain-variant tensor classification tasks for hyperspectral imaging and multivariate time series analysis.
AI.conclusion: Combining structural tensor decomposition with functional adaptability through RKHS representation provides valuable approach for analyzing continuous multidimensional data, particularly beneficial for domain-variant classification tasks.

title: Hypercontractivity type property for generalized Mehler semigroups
summary: We investigate the hypercontractivity property of generalized Mehler semigroups on the $L^p$-scale with respect to invariant measures. This property is first obtained in the purely theoretical setting of skew operators and, subsequently, deduced for generalized Mehler semigroups arising from linear stochastic differential equations perturbed by Lévy noise. When the associated invariant measure $μ$ lacks a purely Gaussian structure, jump components may prevent the validity of Nelson's classical $L^p$-$L^q$ estimates. However, a summability-improving property can be obtained in the setting of mixed-norm spaces $\mathcal{X}_{p,q}(E;γ,π)$ related to the factorization of the invariant measure $μ= γ* π$ into a Gaussian part $γ$ and an infinitely divisible non-Gaussian part $π$. As in the classical Gaussian case, some modified logarithmic Sobolev inequalities with respect to invariant measures can be inferred.

AI.tldr: The paper studies hypercontractivity of generalized Mehler semigroups on L^p spaces with invariant measures, showing that jump components in Lévy noise can break classical Nelson estimates, but summability improvement can be obtained in mixed-norm spaces when the invariant measure factors into Gaussian and non-Gaussian parts.
AI.motivation: To understand hypercontractivity properties of generalized Mehler semigroups arising from linear stochastic differential equations with Lévy noise, particularly when invariant measures are not purely Gaussian and contain jump components.
AI.method: First analyze hypercontractivity in theoretical setting of skew operators, then apply to generalized Mehler semigroups from linear SDEs with Lévy noise. Use mixed-norm spaces related to factorization of invariant measure into Gaussian and infinitely divisible non-Gaussian parts.
AI.result: Jump components can prevent classical Nelson L^p-L^q estimates, but summability-improving property can be obtained in mixed-norm spaces. Modified logarithmic Sobolev inequalities with respect to invariant measures can be inferred, similar to classical Gaussian case.
AI.conclusion: Hypercontractivity properties persist for generalized Mehler semigroups even with non-Gaussian invariant measures, though classical estimates may fail, requiring modified analysis in mixed-norm spaces that account for the measure's Gaussian and non-Gaussian components.

title: Particle method for a nonlinear multimarginal optimal transport problem
summary: We study a nonlinear multimarginal optimal transport problem arising in risk management, where the objective is to maximize a spectral risk measure of the pushforward of a coupling by a cost function. Although this problem is inherently nonlinear, it is known to have an equivalent linear reformulation as a multimarginal transport problem with an additional marginal. We introduce a Lagrangian particle discretization of this problem, in which admissible couplings are approximated by uniformly weighted point clouds, and marginal constraints are enforced through Wasserstein penalization. We prove quantitative convergence results for this discretization as the number of particles tends to infinity. The convergence rate is shown to be governed by the uniform quantization error of an optimal solution, and can be bounded in terms of the geometric properties of its support, notably its box dimension. In the case of univariate marginals and supermodular cost functions, where optimal couplings are known to be comonotone, we obtain sharper convergence rates expressed in terms of the asymptotic quantization errors of the marginals themselves. We also discuss the particular case of conditional value at risk, for which the problem reduces to a multimarginal partial transport formulation. Finally, we illustrate our approach with numerical experiments in several application domains, including risk management and partial barycenters, as well as some artificial examples with a repulsive cost.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a Lagrangian particle discretization method for nonlinear multimarginal optimal transport problems in risk management, proving convergence rates based on quantization errors and geometric properties of solution supports.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to develop efficient computational methods for nonlinear multimarginal optimal transport problems that arise in risk management applications, specifically for maximizing spectral risk measures. These problems are computationally challenging due to their nonlinear nature and high dimensionality.
AI.method: The authors propose a Lagrangian particle discretization approach where admissible couplings are approximated by uniformly weighted point clouds. Marginal constraints are enforced through Wasserstein penalization. The method leverages the known linear reformulation of the nonlinear problem as a multimarginal transport problem with an additional marginal.
AI.result: The paper proves quantitative convergence results as the number of particles increases, showing that convergence rates are governed by uniform quantization error of optimal solutions and can be bounded by geometric properties of solution supports (notably box dimension). For univariate marginals with supermodular costs (where optimal couplings are comonotone), sharper convergence rates are obtained based on asymptotic quantization errors of the marginals themselves.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Lagrangian particle discretization provides a practical computational framework for nonlinear multimarginal optimal transport problems in risk management, with theoretical convergence guarantees. The method is validated through numerical experiments in risk management, partial barycenters, and repulsive cost scenarios, demonstrating its applicability across various domains.

title: Arefinement of the Bukhgeim-Klibanov method
summary: In this article, we improve the classical Bukhgeim-Klibanov method presented in [1],which can be used to prove the conditional stability of inverse source problem for a hyperbolic equation from the measurement on the subboundary. A major ingredient of our proof is a novel Carleman estimate. This inequality eliminates the need to extend the solution in time, therefore simplifies the existing proofs, which is widely applicable to various evolution equations.

AI.tldr: Improved Bukhgeim-Klibanov method for hyperbolic inverse source problems with novel Carleman estimate that eliminates time extension requirement
AI.motivation: To simplify and improve the classical Bukhgeim-Klibanov method for proving conditional stability of inverse source problems in hyperbolic equations from subboundary measurements
AI.method: Develops a novel Carleman estimate that eliminates the need to extend solutions in time, simplifying existing proofs and making the approach applicable to various evolution equations
AI.result: Improved method for proving conditional stability of inverse source problems for hyperbolic equations from subboundary measurements
AI.conclusion: The new Carleman estimate-based approach simplifies the Bukhgeim-Klibanov method, eliminates time extension requirements, and has broad applicability to evolution equations

title: Evolution of the radius of analyticity for mKdV-type equations
summary: In this paper, we obtain new lower bounds for the evolution of the radius of analyticity of solutions to two initial value problems (IVPs) with initial data belonging to the class of analytic functions $H^{σ,s}(\mathbb{R})$ defined via a hyperbolic cosine weight. First, we consider the IVP for the modified Korteweg-de Vries (mKdV) equation. For this problem, we prove that the evolution of the radius of analyticity $σ(T)$ of the solution admits an algebraic lower bound $cT^{-\frac 12}$ for some $c>0$ and given arbitrarily large $T>0$. Next, we analyze the IVP for the mKdV equation with generalized dispersion (mKdVm) and a damping term. For this problem, we guarantee the local well-posedness in $H^{σ,s}(\mathbb{R})$ and demonstrate that the local solution can be extended globally in time and admits constant lower bounds for the radius of analyticity $σ(t)$ as time goes to infinity. The outcome of this paper concerning the mKdV equation represents an improvement on that achieved by the authors' previous work in [R. O. Figueira and M. Panthee, New lower bounds for the radius of analyticity for the mKdV equation and a system of mKdV-type equations, J. Evol. Equ. 24 No. 42 (2024)]. As far as we know, the results for the mKdVm with damping are new.

AI.tldr: New lower bounds for radius of analyticity evolution in mKdV and mKdVm with damping equations, improving previous results.
AI.motivation: To establish better understanding of analyticity preservation in dispersive PDEs, specifically improving lower bounds for radius of analyticity evolution in mKdV-type equations, which has implications for solution regularity and long-time behavior.
AI.method: Analyze two IVPs with analytic initial data in weighted Sobolev spaces H^{σ,s}(ℝ). For mKdV, prove algebraic lower bound cT^{-1/2} for radius of analyticity σ(T). For mKdVm with damping, establish local well-posedness in H^{σ,s}(ℝ), extend solutions globally, and show constant lower bounds for σ(t) as t→∞.
AI.result: For mKdV: improved algebraic lower bound cT^{-1/2} for radius of analyticity evolution. For mKdVm with damping: local well-posedness, global extension, and constant lower bounds for analyticity radius as t→∞. The mKdV result improves authors' previous work; mKdVm with damping results are new.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides improved quantitative understanding of analyticity preservation in mKdV-type equations, with mKdV showing algebraic decay of analyticity radius and mKdVm with damping maintaining constant analyticity bounds, demonstrating damping's stabilizing effect on analytic properties.

title: Wave-Current-Bathymetry Interaction Revisited: Modeling, Analysis and Asymptotics
summary: Surface gravity waves propagating over variable currents and bathymetry are studied in the linear regime. The leading-order problem is formulated in terms of surface variables, surface current, and the bathymetry-dependent Dirichlet-to-Neumann (DN) operator. Well-posedness of the governing PDE is then established using the theory of hyperbolic systems of pseudo-differential operators. The asymptotic analysis of waves in a slowly varying environment is subsequently considered: the semiclassical Weyl quantization of the symbol $g_b(X,ξ)=|ξ|\tanh(b(X)|ξ|)$ is shown to be both asymptotically accurate and consistent with the self-adjoint structure of the DN operator, and key properties needed for the asymptotic analysis are derived. The energy dynamics are then examined, leading to a novel equation for the evolution of total wave energy. Moreover, the asymptotic surface model based on the Weyl quantization of the DN operator is shown to recover well-known asymptotic models such as the wave action equation, the mild-slope equation, the Schrödinger equation, and the action balance equation, thereby providing a unified framework for the linear theory of wave-current-bathymetry interaction. Several numerical experiments are included throughout to illustrate the theoretical results.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a unified mathematical framework for linear surface gravity waves interacting with variable currents and bathymetry using pseudo-differential operator theory and Weyl quantization.
AI.motivation: To establish a rigorous mathematical foundation for studying surface gravity waves in variable environments (currents and bathymetry) that unifies existing asymptotic models and provides well-posedness guarantees.
AI.method: Uses hyperbolic systems of pseudo-differential operators to prove well-posedness, employs semiclassical Weyl quantization of the Dirichlet-to-Neumann operator symbol, and develops asymptotic analysis for slowly varying environments.
AI.result: Established well-posedness of governing PDEs, derived novel energy evolution equation, showed Weyl quantization recovers known asymptotic models (wave action, mild-slope, Schrödinger, action balance equations), and validated with numerical experiments.
AI.conclusion: Provides a unified mathematical framework for linear wave-current-bathymetry interaction that is rigorous, asymptotically accurate, and connects various established models through pseudo-differential operator theory.

title: Sharp bounds and geometric properties of the first non trivial Steklov Neumann Eigenvalue
summary: In this article, we study the mixed Steklov--Neumann eigenvalue problem on doubly connected domains. First, we show that among all doubly connected domains in $\mathbb{R}^n$ of the form $B_{R_2}\setminus \overline{B_{R_1}}$, where $B_{R_1}$ and $B_{R_2}$ are open balls of fixed radii satisfying $\overline{B_{R_1}} \subset B_{R_2}$, the first non-zero Steklov--Neumann eigenvalue attains its maximal value when the balls are concentric. Next, we establish bounds for the first non-zero Steklov--Neumann eigenvalue on a doubly connected star-shaped domain contained in a hypersurface equipped with a revolution-type metric. We also derive the asymptotic behavior of the first non-zero Steklov--Neumann eigenvalue on a bounded domain with a spherical hole in $\mathbb{R}^n$ as the radius of the hole approaches zero. Finally, we study the number of nodal domains of the eigenfunction corresponding to the first non zero Steklov--Neumann eigenvalue on a bounded domain in $\mathbb{R}^n$ having a spherical hole.

AI.tldr: The paper studies mixed Steklov-Neumann eigenvalue problems on doubly connected domains, proving maximality for concentric balls, establishing bounds for star-shaped domains, analyzing asymptotic behavior as holes shrink, and studying nodal domains.
AI.motivation: To understand the spectral properties of mixed Steklov-Neumann eigenvalue problems on doubly connected domains, which arise in various physical and mathematical contexts involving boundary value problems with mixed boundary conditions.
AI.method: Analytical and geometric methods including variational characterization of eigenvalues, comparison techniques, asymptotic analysis, and nodal domain theory applied to doubly connected domains with mixed boundary conditions.
AI.result: 1) First non-zero Steklov-Neumann eigenvalue is maximized for concentric balls among annular domains; 2) Bounds established for star-shaped domains with revolution-type metrics; 3) Asymptotic behavior derived as hole radius approaches zero; 4) Nodal domain properties studied for domains with spherical holes.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides comprehensive results on mixed Steklov-Neumann eigenvalues for doubly connected domains, establishing extremal properties, bounds, asymptotic behavior, and nodal domain characteristics, contributing to the understanding of spectral geometry with mixed boundary conditions.

title: From nonisothermal BGK to Euler Maxwellians via relative entropy
summary: We study the hydrodynamic limit of the nonisothermal BGK model toward smooth Euler Maxwellians. For a prescribed smooth Euler solution, we derive a relative entropy stability estimate between a BGK solution and the associated Maxwellian. The main new ingredient is the control of an additional velocity-cubic term in the relative entropy identity. Under a uniform sixth velocity-moment bound and suitable bounds on the BGK macroscopic quantities, we obtain a uniform-in-time relative entropy estimate. For well-prepared initial data, this yields strong $L^1$ convergence of the BGK solution and the local Maxwellians to the target Euler Maxwellian, together with convergence of the associated macroscopic quantities.

AI.tldr: The paper establishes hydrodynamic limit of nonisothermal BGK model to Euler equations via relative entropy method, achieving strong L¹ convergence with well-prepared initial data.
AI.motivation: To rigorously justify the hydrodynamic limit from kinetic theory (BGK model) to fluid dynamics (Euler equations) in the nonisothermal setting, which is important for connecting microscopic and macroscopic descriptions of gas dynamics.
AI.method: Uses relative entropy method with careful control of velocity-cubic terms in the entropy identity. Requires uniform sixth velocity-moment bounds and suitable bounds on BGK macroscopic quantities to obtain uniform-in-time estimates.
AI.result: Obtains uniform-in-time relative entropy estimate between BGK solution and Euler Maxwellian. For well-prepared initial data, proves strong L¹ convergence of BGK solution and local Maxwellians to target Euler Maxwellian, along with convergence of macroscopic quantities.
AI.conclusion: The relative entropy method successfully establishes the hydrodynamic limit from nonisothermal BGK model to Euler equations, providing rigorous mathematical justification for this important connection between kinetic and fluid descriptions.

title: Uniform estimates and Brezis-Merle type inequalities for the $k$-Hessian equation
summary: In this paper, we prove a Brezis-Merle type inequality for $k$-convex functions vanishing on the boundary. As an application, we establish an Alexandrov-Bakelman-Pucci type estimate for the intermediate Hessian equation. Furthermore, we establish a concentration-compactness principle for the blow-up behavior of solutions to the Liouville type $k$-Hessian equations.

AI.tldr: The paper proves a Brezis-Merle type inequality for k-convex functions, establishes an Alexandrov-Bakelman-Pucci type estimate for intermediate Hessian equations, and develops a concentration-compactness principle for blow-up behavior in k-Hessian Liouville equations.
AI.motivation: To extend classical elliptic PDE results (Brezis-Merle inequality, Alexandrov-Bakelman-Pucci estimates, concentration-compactness principles) from the Laplacian case to the more general k-Hessian operator setting, addressing the analysis of k-convex functions and their associated equations.
AI.method: The authors develop analytical techniques for k-convex functions, proving a Brezis-Merle type inequality for functions vanishing on boundaries. They then apply this to derive Alexandrov-Bakelman-Pucci type estimates for intermediate Hessian equations. Finally, they establish a concentration-compactness principle to analyze blow-up behavior in Liouville-type k-Hessian equations.
AI.result: Three main results: (1) A Brezis-Merle type inequality for k-convex functions with boundary conditions, (2) An Alexandrov-Bakelman-Pucci type estimate for intermediate Hessian equations, and (3) A concentration-compactness principle describing blow-up behavior in k-Hessian Liouville equations.
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully extends important analytical tools from second-order elliptic theory to the k-Hessian framework, providing new inequalities, estimates, and compactness principles that advance the understanding of k-convex functions and their associated PDEs.

title: A new formula for the Wasserstein distance between solutions to (nonlinear) continuity equations
summary: Given two continuity equations with density-dependent velocities, we provide a new formula for the Wasserstein distance between the solutions in terms of the difference of velocities evaluated at the same density. The formula is particularly attractive to deduce quantitative estimates and rates of convergence for singular limits. We illustrate it using several examples. For the porous medium equation with exponent $m$, we prove that solutions are Lipschitz continuous with respect to $m$, providing a quantitative version of the result of Bénilan and Crandall. This result can be extended to a general aggregation-diffusion equation. We also study the limit $m \to \infty$ (the so-called mesa problem or the incompressible limit) and we recover the rate of convergence $1/{\sqrt{m}}$. Last but not least, we improve the rate of nonlocal-to-local convergence for the quadratic porous medium equation from recently obtained $\sqrt{\varepsilon}$ to numerically conjectured $\varepsilon$.

AI.tldr: New formula for Wasserstein distance between solutions of continuity equations with density-dependent velocities, enabling quantitative estimates for singular limits with applications to porous medium equation and aggregation-diffusion equations.
AI.motivation: Need quantitative estimates and rates of convergence for singular limits in continuity equations with density-dependent velocities, particularly for porous medium equation and related models where existing results lack precise convergence rates.
AI.method: Develops new formula for Wasserstein distance between solutions in terms of velocity differences evaluated at same density. Applies this formula to analyze: 1) Lipschitz continuity of porous medium solutions with respect to exponent m, 2) mesa problem (m→∞) convergence rate, 3) nonlocal-to-local convergence for quadratic porous medium equation.
AI.result: 1) Proves Lipschitz continuity of porous medium solutions with respect to exponent m (quantitative version of Bénilan-Crandall result). 2) Recovers 1/√m convergence rate for mesa problem (incompressible limit). 3) Improves nonlocal-to-local convergence rate from √ε to ε for quadratic porous medium equation, matching numerical conjectures.
AI.conclusion: The new Wasserstein distance formula provides powerful tool for obtaining quantitative convergence rates in singular limits of continuity equations, with applications to porous medium equation, aggregation-diffusion equations, and nonlocal-to-local limits, yielding optimal or improved rates.

title: Bubbling of almost critical points of anisotropic isoperimetric problems with degenerating ellipticity
summary: Given a sequence of uniformly convex norms $ φ_h $ on $ \mathbf{R}^{n+1} $ converging to an arbitrary norm $ φ$, we prove rigidity of $ L^1 $-accumulation points of sequences of sets $ E_h \subseteq \mathbf{R}^{n+1} $ of finite perimeter, that are volume-constrained almost-critical points of the anisotropic surface energy functionals associated with $ φ_h $. Here, almost criticality is measured in terms of the $ L^n $-deviation from being constant of the distributional anisotropic mean $ φ_h $-curvature of (the varifold associated to) of the reduced boundaries of $ E_h $. We prove that such limits are finite union of disjoint, but possibly mutually tangent, $ φ$-Wulff shapes.

AI.tldr: Rigidity theorem: L¹-accumulation points of sequences of volume-constrained almost-critical sets for anisotropic surface energies are finite unions of disjoint (possibly tangent) Wulff shapes.
AI.motivation: Study rigidity properties of sets that are almost-critical points of anisotropic surface energies, extending classical results for isotropic cases to anisotropic settings with converging norms.
AI.method: Analyze sequences of sets with finite perimeter that are volume-constrained almost-critical points of anisotropic surface energies, measuring criticality via Lⁿ-deviation of anisotropic mean curvature from constant.
AI.result: Prove that L¹-accumulation points of such sequences are finite unions of disjoint (possibly mutually tangent) φ-Wulff shapes, where φ is the limit norm.
AI.conclusion: Establishes strong rigidity for almost-critical sets in anisotropic settings, showing they must be composed of Wulff shapes even under weak convergence assumptions.

title: A non-Kähler expanding Ricci soliton with a Kähler tangent cone at infinity
summary: We construct an example of an asymptotically conical (AC) non-Kähler expanding gradient Ricci soliton that has a Kähler tangent cone at infinity. This yields an example of a Kähler cone that can be desingularised by a smooth AC expanding gradient Ricci soliton but not by a smooth AC expanding gradient Kähler--Ricci soliton.

AI.tldr: Constructed an asymptotically conical non-Kähler expanding gradient Ricci soliton with Kähler tangent cone at infinity, showing some Kähler cones can be desingularized by non-Kähler solitons but not by Kähler ones.
AI.motivation: To investigate the relationship between Kähler cones and their possible desingularizations via expanding gradient Ricci solitons, particularly exploring whether Kähler cones can only be desingularized by Kähler solitons or if non-Kähler solitons can also serve this purpose.
AI.method: Constructed an explicit example of an asymptotically conical (AC) expanding gradient Ricci soliton that is non-Kähler but has a Kähler tangent cone at its asymptotic infinity.
AI.result: Successfully constructed the first example of a non-Kähler expanding gradient Ricci soliton that asymptotically approaches a Kähler cone, demonstrating that some Kähler cones can be desingularized by smooth AC expanding gradient Ricci solitons that are not Kähler.
AI.conclusion: This provides a counterexample showing that Kähler cones can be desingularized by non-Kähler expanding gradient Ricci solitons, establishing that the property of being Kähler is not necessary for such desingularizations, even when the asymptotic cone is Kähler.

title: WKB for semiclassical operators: How to fly over caustics (and more)
comment: 32 pages. 100th anniversary of the WKB papers!
summary: The method initiated by Wentzel, Kramers, and Brillouin to find approximate solutions to the Schrödinger equation lies at the origin of the spectacular development of microlocal and semiclassical analysis. When used naively, the approach appears to break down at caustics, but Maslov showed how a simple generalization could overcome this difficulty. In this paper, after a partial historical review, we take advantage of more recent advances in microlocal analysis to present a unified treatment of this generalized Maslov-WKB method, using a microlocal sheaf-theoretic approach. This framework provides a rigorous proof of the Bohr Sommerfeld Einstein Brillouin Keller quantization conditions for the eigenvalues of general semiclassical operators (pseudodifferential and Berezin Toeplitz) in one degree of freedom. We also review some applications and extensions.

AI.tldr: A unified microlocal sheaf-theoretic treatment of the generalized Maslov-WKB method for semiclassical analysis, providing rigorous proof of BSEBK quantization conditions.
AI.motivation: The WKB method is fundamental to microlocal/semiclassical analysis but breaks down at caustics. Maslov's generalization addressed this, and recent advances in microlocal analysis enable a unified treatment.
AI.method: Uses a microlocal sheaf-theoretic approach to present a unified treatment of the generalized Maslov-WKB method, building on recent advances in microlocal analysis.
AI.result: Provides rigorous proof of Bohr-Sommerfeld-Einstein-Brillouin-Keller (BSEBK) quantization conditions for eigenvalues of general semiclassical operators (pseudodifferential and Berezin-Toeplitz) in one degree of freedom.
AI.conclusion: The microlocal sheaf-theoretic framework successfully unifies the Maslov-WKB method and rigorously establishes quantization conditions, with applications and extensions reviewed.
